mnemonics.title: Agile Values Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Remember IVWW: Individuals & interactions, Working software, Customer collaboration, Responding to change - think 'I View Willy's Walrus' as a funny phrase to remember the order.

mnemonics.title: Scrum Roles Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: PSP - Product Owner (Prioritizes), Scrum Master (Supports), Programmers (Produce). Or remember: 'Please Serve Product' - Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team.

mnemonics.title: Scrum Events Sequence
mnemonics.content: SPRINT: Start → Sprint Planning → Daily Scrum → Sprint Review → Sprint Retrospective → End. Remember: 'Please Don't Question Sprints Require Testing' - Planning, Daily, Q&A (Review), Sprint, Retrospective.

mnemonics.title: Agile Principles First Letters
mnemonics.content: C-W-I-S-F-C-D-E-T-P: Customer satisfaction, Welcome changes, Working software frequently, Business and developers together, Motivated individuals, Face-to-face conversation, Working software is measure, Sustainable development, Continuous attention to technical excellence, Simplicity, Self-organizing teams, Regular reflection and adjustment.

acronyms.usage: The smallest version of a product that can be released to validate learning and gather feedback from customers.
In Kanban, limiting WIP helps identify bottlenecks and improves workflow efficiency.
An XP practice where tests are written before code, then code is written to pass those tests.
Practices in Agile where code changes are automatically integrated and deployed to production.
A person with deep knowledge in a particular domain, often consulted during Agile development.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a waterfall: one arrow flowing straight down through phases. Draw Agile: a circular staircase or series of circles (iterations) going upward, each cycle incorporating feedback arrows going back.

Picture a circle divided into Sprint Planning (top left), Daily Scrum (left), Sprint Review (bottom), Sprint Retrospective (right), all within a Sprint boundary. Product Backlog feeds into Planning, Sprint Backlog is the work, Increment comes out.
Think of a 3-part equation: As a [WHO] + I want [WHAT] + So that [WHY]. Write it like: USER → ACTION → BENEFIT.
Draw a circular cycle showing: Plan → Build → Test → Review → Retro → Repeat. Each rotation builds on the previous, spiraling upward in quality.

analogies.analogy: Think of building with LEGO bricks. Instead of planning the entire castle first and building it all at once, you build one section, show it to others, get feedback, then continue building and improving each section iteratively.

Like a restaurant menu. The Chef (Product Owner) decides what dishes (features) to add, prioritizes them based on customer (customer) preferences, and the kitchen team (Development Team) prepares them in order.
Like a sports team huddle before a game. Quick 15-second updates from each player: what they did, what they'll do next, and any blockers (injuries/problems) that need attention.
Like a post-game team meeting. The team discusses what went well (victories), what didn't (mistakes), and what to practice/improve for the next game (Sprint).
